target.name: alanense A
target.motivation: Alanenses A and B inhibit spontaneous calcium channel oscillations (SCOs) at low micromolar concentrations. The aromatic group at C1- and C2-positions is essential for inhibitory activity.

success_criteria.free_text: Total synthesis of alanense A and B, and O70-methyllacinilene E, demonstrating a deoxygenative arylation strategy.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond between C1 of cadinane and C1 of catechol
key_disconnections.reasoning: Inspired by a biosynthetic hypothesis involving a radical intermediate, the authors envisioned a 'contra-biosynthetic' approach to form the C-C bond via deoxygenative arylation of a lacinilene C derivative.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy involves installing an NPIB group onto a tertiary alcohol of a lacinilene C derivative. This NPIB derivative would then serve as a radical precursor for deoxygenative arylation with a catechol derivative. The initial photoredox approach was later supplemented by an acid-mediated approach due to reactivity issues with the target molecule.

thought.background: Aryl iodide 17 is a commercially available starting material. Regioselective reverse-prenylation is a known reaction.
thought.gap: To introduce the prenyl group at the desired position of aryl iodide 17.
thought.decision: To achieve regioselective reverse-prenylation, used prenylboronic ester 18 with Pd(PPh3)4 and CsF. Expected to form coupled product 19.

outcome.observation: Coupled product 19 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Our synthesis commenced with a regioselective reverse-prenylation of commercially available aryl iodide 17 using prenylboronic ester 1824 in the presence of Pd(PPh3)4 and CsF25 to afford coupled product 19 in 46% yield (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Terminal alkene 19 is available. Wickens' radical hydrocarboxylation is a known method for homologation.
thought.gap: To homologate the terminal alkene 19 to a carboxylic acid.
thought.decision: To produce homologated carboxylic acid 20, employed Wickens' radical hydrocarboxylation. Expected to form carboxylic acid 20.

outcome.observation: Homologated carboxylic acid 20 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: With terminal alkene 19 in hand, we then employed Wickens' radical hydrocarboxylation to produce homologated carboxylic acid 20 in 71% yield.

thought.background: Carboxylic acid 20 is available. McCormick's protocol is known for forming the cadinane framework.
thought.gap: To transform carboxylic acid 20 into the cadinane framework.
thought.decision: To form the cadinane framework 22, used McCormick's protocol. Expected to form 22.

outcome.observation: Compound 21 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, carboxylic acid 20 was transformed into cadinane framework 22 based on McCormick's protocol.27

thought.background: Compound 21 is available. The next step in McCormick's protocol involves cyclization.
thought.gap: To complete the formation of the cadinane framework.
thought.decision: To complete the cadinane framework, used MeMgI followed by aqueous HCl. Expected to form 22.

outcome.observation: Compound 22 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, carboxylic acid 20 was transformed into cadinane framework 22 based on McCormick's protocol.27

thought.background: Compound 22 is available. Epoxidation and oxidative epoxide opening are known reactions to introduce hydroxyl groups.
thought.gap: To introduce a hydroxyl group and form the naphthol derivative 23.
thought.decision: To form 2-hydroxy-7-methoxycadalene (23), performed epoxidation of 22 followed by an oxidative epoxide opening reaction. Expected to form 23.

outcome.observation: Natural 2-hydroxy-7-methoxycadalene (23) was obtained over two steps.

evidence.text_span: Epoxidation of compound 22 followed by an oxidative epoxide opening reaction afforded natural 2-hydroxy-7- methoxycadalene (23) in 38% yield over two steps.

thought.background: Naphthol derivative 23 is available. Oxone-mediated dearomatization is a known method to form alpha-hydroxyketones.
thought.gap: To dearomatize 23 and form the alpha-hydroxyketone 8.
thought.decision: To form alpha-hydroxyketone 8, used Oxone-mediated dearomatization of naphthol derivative 23. Expected to form 8.

outcome.observation: Alpha-hydroxyketone 8 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Oxonemediated dearomatization of naphthol derivative 23 yielded ahydroxyketone 8 in 58% yield.

thought.background: Compound 8 has a tertiary alcohol. NPIB acid 9 is the redox-active handle to be installed.
thought.gap: To esterify the tertiary alcohol of 8 with NPIB acid 9.
thought.decision: To install the NPIB group, performed esterification of 8 with alpha-N-phthalimido-oxy isobutyric acid (9) using EDC-HCl and DMAP. Expected to form NPIB derivative 10.

outcome.observation: NPIB derivative 10 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Esterication of the tertiary alcohol group in compound 8 with a-N-phthalimido-oxy isobutyric acid (9) was achieved in 82% yield in the presence of EDC$HCl and DMAP.

thought.background: NPIB derivative 10 is ready for deoxygenative arylation. The standard photoredox catalytic conditions were previously optimized for a model system.
thought.gap: To perform the deoxygenative arylation with 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) using the established photoredox conditions.
thought.decision: Attempted deoxygenative arylation of NPIB derivative 10 with 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) under standard photoredox conditions. Expected to form the arylated product.

outcome.observation: An intractable mixture of products was observed.

evidence.text_span: However, an intractable mixture of products was observed when NPIB derivative 10 and 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) were subjected to the aforementioned standard photoredox catalytic conditions (Scheme 4).

thought.background: The photoredox conditions failed due to a polarity mismatch in the C-C bond formation. DFT calculations supported this, showing a higher activation barrier for the target system compared to the model system.
thought.gap: To find an alternative method for deoxygenative arylation that overcomes the polarity mismatch.
thought.decision: To address the reactivity issue, explored acid-mediated conditions. Discovered that NPIB derivative 10 and 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) could be coupled in the presence of TFA in HFIP. Expected to form product 24.

outcome.observation: Product 24 was obtained in high yield. This acid-mediated approach was successful.

evidence.text_span: We speculated that the increased electron density of the $\alpha$-keto radical intermediate and the decreased electron density of the aromatic coupling partner caused a polarity mismatch during the key C–C bond formation. In fact, DFT calculations revealed that the activation barrier for the C–C coupling between radical intermediate B (Scheme 3B) and 1,2,4-trimethoxybenzene is 27.1 kcal mol−1, while the activation energy for the coupling between radical 11 and 1,2-dimethoxybenzene is 32.4 kcal mol−1 (for details, see Fig. S4†). Extensive experimentations were conducted with a model system to remedy the observed lack of desired reactivity under our previously optimized photocatalytic reaction conditions. To our delight, we discovered that NPIB derivative 10 and 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) could be coupled in the presence of 5 equiv. of TFA in hexauoroisopropanol (HFIP) to yield product 24 in 98% yield (vide infra).

thought.background: Trimethylated compound 24 is available. Demethylation is required to obtain alanense A.
thought.gap: To demethylate compound 24 to yield alanense A (3).
thought.decision: To achieve demethylation, treated compound 24 with BBr3. Expected to form alanense A (3).

outcome.observation: Synthetic sample of alanense A (3) was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Finally, treatment of trimethylated compound 24 with 10 equiv. of BBr3 afforded the synthetic sample of alanense A (3) in 59% yield.

thought.background: Ketone precursor 21 is available. Alpha-methylation and alpha-hydroxylation are needed to prepare for alanense B synthesis.
thought.gap: To introduce a methyl group and a hydroxyl group at the alpha position of the ketone.
thought.decision: To prepare alcohol 26, performed alpha-methylation of 21 using LiHMDS and MeI, followed by alpha-hydroxylation using TBD, Cu2O, and O2. Expected to form 26.

outcome.observation: Alcohol 26 was obtained over two steps.

evidence.text_span: a-Methylation of common ketone precursor 21 and subsequent $\alpha$-hydroxylation of the resulting methylated product via a protocol reported by the Schoenebeck group34 afforded alcohol 26 in 53% yield over two steps (Scheme 6).

thought.background: Alcohol 26 is available. DDQ oxidation is a known method for forming conjugated alcohols.
thought.gap: To oxidize alcohol 26 to conjugated alcohol 27.
thought.decision: To form conjugated alcohol 27, oxidized compound 26 with DDQ. Expected to form 27.

outcome.observation: Conjugated alcohol 27 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Oxidation of compound 26 with DDQ produced conjugated alcohol 27 in 57% yield.

thought.background: Alcohol 27 is available. NPIB acid 9 needs to be installed.
thought.gap: To esterify alcohol 27 with NPIB acid 9.
thought.decision: To form NPIB derivative 28, treated alcohol 27 with NPIB acid 9 in the presence of EDC-HCl and DMAP. Expected to form 28 as a single regioisomer.

outcome.observation: NPIB derivative 28 was obtained as a single regioisomer, but partially isomerized to 29 during silica gel column chromatography. 28 (59% yield) and 29 (10% yield) were isolated.

evidence.text_span: Treatment of alcohol 27 with NPIB acid 9 in the presence of EDC$HCl and DMAP forged NPIB derivative 28 as a single regioisomer. However, NPIB derivative 28 was partially isomerized to 29 during silica gel column chromatography to yield 28 and 29 in 59% and 10% yield, respectively.

thought.background: NPIB derivative 28 is available. The acid-mediated deoxygenative arylation conditions were successful for alanense A synthesis.
thought.gap: To perform the deoxygenative arylation with 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) for alanense B synthesis.
thought.decision: Attempted deoxygenative arylation of 28 with 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) using TFA in HFIP. Expected to form arylated product 30.

outcome.observation: Desired arylated product 30 was obtained in low yield (19%) along with a significant amount of [3+2] cycloaddition product 31 (17%). When 20 equiv. of TFA was used, only 31 was obtained (43% yield).

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, treatment of 28 with 1,2-dimethoxybenzene (7b) and 5 equiv. of TFA in HFIP solution yielded desired arylated product 30 (19%) along with [3+2] cycloaddition product 31 (17%), likely formed via cationic intermediate 32. When 28 was allowed to react with 20 equiv. of TFA in HFIP, only cycloaddition product 31 was obtained in 43% yield. The structure of [3+2] cycloaddition product 31 was unambiguously conrmed by a single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis. It is notable that the bicyclo[3.2.1]octadienone framework in 31 constitutes the backbone of various natural products including naphthocyclinone.35 The subjection of the NPIB regioisomer 29 to these reaction conditions revealed analogous reaction outcomes.

thought.background: The previous attempt resulted in an undesired cycloaddition product. The intramolecular Friedel-Crafts reaction was problematic.
thought.gap: To prevent the intramolecular cycloaddition and achieve selective arylation.
thought.decision: To circumvent the formation of the [3+2] cycloaddition byproduct, selected phenol (7x) as the coupling partner. Reasoned that the meta position of phenol is deactivated, preventing intramolecular cyclization. Expected to form alpha-arylated product 33 exclusively.

outcome.observation: Desired alpha-arylated product 33 was exclusively formed (66% from 28, 63% from 29). Nucleophilic phenol addition at the alpha-position was kinetically favored.

evidence.text_span: To circumvent the formation of the [3+2] cycloaddition byproduct, phenol (7x) was selected as the coupling partner. We reasoned that the undesired intramolecular Friedel–Cras reaction-based cyclization would not be feasible because the meta position of the phenol group is deactivated by an inductively electron-withdrawing oxygen atom. This strategic design was possible owing to the broad substrate scope of the newly developed TFA/HFIP-based arylation reaction. In the event, when NPIB derivative 28 (or 29) was allowed to react with phenol in the presence of TFA (5 equiv.) in HFIP, the desired $\alpha$-arylated product 33 was exclusively formed in 66% yield (63% yield from 29). The exclusive arylation at the $\alpha$-position of the ketone group is noteworthy. Based on DFT calculations, nucleophilic phenol addition at the $\alpha$-position is kinetically favored ($\Delta G^\ddagger$ = 14.5 kcal mol−1) over addition at the benzylic position ($\Delta G^\ddagger$ = 17.2 kcal mol−1), presumably due to the signicant steric hindrance posed by the benzylic isopropyl substituent (for details, see Fig. S7†).

thought.background: Phenol derivative 33 is available. Oxidation to an ortho-quinone and subsequent reduction are needed to form the catechol derivative.
thought.gap: To convert the phenol 33 to catechol derivative 34.
thought.decision: To form catechol derivative 34, oxidized 33 to the ortho-quinone with IBX, followed by one-pot addition of aqueous sodium dithionite. Expected to form 34.

outcome.observation: Catechol derivative 34 was obtained. Chloroform/methanol cosolvent was critical.

evidence.text_span: For the endgame of the synthesis, phenol derivative 33 was oxidized to the ortho-quinone derivative in the presence of IBX at $-25~^\circ\mathrm{C}$. Subsequent one-pot addition of aqueous sodium dithionite solution to the reaction mixture resulted in catechol derivative 34 in 71% yield. The use of chloroform/methanol cosolvent was critical for the efficient transformation.

thought.background: Catechol derivative 34 is available. Demethylation of the methoxy group is the final step to alanense B.
thought.gap: To demethylate the methoxy group in 34 to yield alanense B (4).
thought.decision: To achieve demethylation, treated 34 with ethanethiol and sodium hydride in DMF. Expected to form alanense B (4).

outcome.observation: The first synthetic sample of alanense B (4) was obtained.

evidence.text_span: The nal demethylation of the methoxy group in 34 was achieved by treating it with ethanethiol and sodium hydride in DMF to produce the rst synthetic sample of alanense B (4) in 48% yield.

thought.background: 2-hydroxy-7-methoxycadalene (23) is available. NPIB derivative 35a is the coupling partner.
thought.gap: To apply the acid-mediated deoxygenative arylation to synthesize lacinilene E core.
thought.decision: Attempted reaction of 23 with NPIB derivative 35a in the presence of TFA in HFIP. Expected to form a coupled product.

outcome.observation: Only decomposition of NPIB derivative 35a was observed. Speculated that electron-rich 35a acts as a dominant nucleophile.

evidence.text_span: To this end, 2-hydroxy-7- methoxycadalene (23) was allowed to react with NPIB derivative 35a in the presence of TFA in HFIP. However, only the decomposition of NPIB derivative 35a was observed under these reaction conditions. We speculated that the electron-rich NPIB derivative 35a acts as a dominant nucleophile over 2-hydroxy-7- methoxycadalene (23).

thought.background: The previous attempt failed due to the nucleophilicity of the NPIB derivative. Fluorination could temper this reactivity.
thought.gap: To temper the nucleophilic reactivity of the NPIB derivative and achieve successful coupling.
thought.decision: Designed fluorinated NPIB derivatives 35b and 35c. Reacted 23 with 35b and 35c in the presence of TFA in HFIP. Expected to form coupled products 36 and 37.

outcome.observation: Coupled products 36 (50% yield) and 37 (32% yield) were formed.

evidence.text_span: To temper the nucleophilic reactivity of the NPIB derivative, we designed uorinated NPIB derivatives 35b and 35c. To our delight, when 23 was allowed to react with uorinated NPIB derivatives 35b and 35c in the presence of TFA in HFIP, coupled products 36 and 37 were formed in 50% and 32% yield, respectively.

thought.background: Coupled product 37 is available. Hydrodefluorination and oxidation are needed to prepare for lacinilene E derivative.
thought.gap: To remove the fluorine and oxidize the alcohol to a ketone.
thought.decision: To form dimethylated lacinilene E derivative 38, performed NiCl2-catalyzed hydrodefluorination using superhydride, followed by DMP-mediated oxidation of the resulting allylic alcohol. Expected to form 38.

outcome.observation: Dimethylated lacinilene E derivative 38 was obtained over two steps. The defluorinated product of 36 was not observed under various conditions.

evidence.text_span: NiCl2-catalyzed hydrodeuorination using superhydride as a reductant37 afforded the deuorinated allylic alcohol product from compound 37. Subsequent DMPmediated oxidation of the resulting alcohol resulted in dimethylated lacinilene E derivative 38 (67% yield over two steps). Markedly, the deuorinated product of compound 36 was not observed under the various conditions tested.

thought.background: Compound 38 is a dimethylated lacinilene E derivative. Demethylation at the O7' position is required to obtain lacinilene E.
thought.gap: To selectively demethylate the O7' position of 38.
thought.decision: Attempted demethylation of 38 with boron tribromide. Expected to form lacinilene E (2).

outcome.observation: Demethylation at O7' was extremely challenging. A trace amount of lacinilene E (2) was obtained along with 2,7-dihydroxycadalene (5) as a major product. Reasoned that demethylation induced C-C bond cleavage via ortho-quinone methide formation.

evidence.text_span: Demethylation at the O7' position of compound 38 was extremely challenging. 2,7-dihydroxycadalene (5) was obtained as a major product under various demethylation conditions used. We reasoned that the demethylation at O7' induced the C–C bond cleavage via the formation of an ortho-quinone methide. In fact, when compound 38 was reacted with boron tribromide as a demethylating agent, a trace amount of lacinilene E (2) was obtained along with 2,7-dihydroxycadalene as a major product (for details, see Fig. S1†).

thought.background: Selective demethylation of O7' was problematic, leading to C-C bond cleavage. The goal is to obtain O7'-methyllacinilene E.
thought.gap: To achieve selective demethylation or obtain the methylated congener.
thought.decision: To obtain O7'-methyllacinilene E (39), subjected compound 38 to ethanethiol and sodium hydride in DMF. Expected to form 39.

outcome.observation: Methylated congener O7'-methyllacinilene E (39) was obtained in 61% yield, along with 2,7-dihydroxycadalene (5) in 23% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subjection of compound 38 to ethanethiol and sodium hydride in DMF delivered the methylated congener O7'-methyllacinilene E (39) in 61% yield along with 2,7-dihydroxycadalene (5) in 23% yield.

validation.significance: The total syntheses of alanenses A and B, and O7'-methyllacinilene E were achieved using novel deoxygenative arylation reactions. The work highlights the versatility of the NPIB group and the effectiveness of TFA/HFIP conditions for challenging Friedel-Crafts reactions, particularly for generating carbocations adjacent to electron-withdrawing groups.

source.title: α-<i>N</i>-phthalimido-oxy isobutyrate-mediated deoxygenative arylation: total synthesis of alanenses A and B.
source.authors: Hyun YE, Kweon J, Linh Phan TH, Kim D, Han S.
